name: Hazel Scott
abstract: Hazel Dorothy Scott (June 11, 1920 – October 2, 1981) was an American jazz and classical pianist and singer. An outspoken critic of racial discrimination and segregation, she used her influence to improve the representation of Black Americans in film.

about_quotes.speaker: Red Callender
about_quotes.evidence: I worked in the movie studios way back in the early Forties... with Hazel Scott and Lee Young... Lee and I were featured with Hazel Scott in that particular picture

about_quotes.speaker: Leonidas Raymond Young
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Hazel Scott ... in a picture with Red Skelton called 'I Doed It'

about_quotes.speaker: Dorothy Donegan
about_quotes.evidence: And she and Hazel Scott were good friends. Did you know Hazel Scott? ... Hazel Scott was a good entertainer. She looked beautiful at the piano. ... Art Tatum had a cutting contest with Hazel Scott and Erroll Garner.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dorothy Donegan — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: We never played behind Hazel when she did her act, but one time I suppose she wanted to see what quality the band really had because she made an arrangement on a song that she wanted us to accompany her [on]...

about_quotes.speaker: Buddy Tate
about_quotes.evidence: Hazel Scott got me interested in it. When I first saw it, nobody was living on there but she and Adam Clayton Powell. So she got me to come up and look at this property and I bought it

about_quotes.speaker: Marian McPartland
about_quotes.evidence: I only knew there were some women musicians that I’d listened to, that I had in the back of my mind that were great women, like Mary Lou and Hazel Scott
about_quotes.doc_title: Marian McPartland — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
